25.1.label: Question 25.1
25.1.scenario:
An actuary will seek to support a health and care insurance company in achieving its aims, within
   the context of the insurance company’s risk profile and its available resources.
25.1.question: State the problems that confront the actuary in doing this.

The problems that confront the actuary a:           Policy data                                                                                    [¼]
The insurance company may not provide data that are complete and accurate.                    [½]
          Product design                                                                                 [¼]
The actuary should consider what contracts the insurance company should offer and what
benefits and design features they should have, given the insurance company’s risk profile
and the resources available to it.                                                     [½]
          Product marketability                                                                          [¼]
The insurance company should optimise the balance between price and volumes of
business (both new business and renewing business for short‐term insurance contracts),
... taking into account competition.                                                          [½]
          Pricing                                                                                        [¼]
Actuaries will need to determine the expected profit from selling a new contract at
particular premium rates or with particular charges, …                                        [½]
… as well as the variance of that profit.                                                     [½]
They will also need to consider whether the insurance company will have the resources to
sell the contract on those terms.                                                    [½]
          Return on capital                                                                              [¼]
The actuary must estimate what return the insurance company expects to make by
investing its capital in the development and issue of a new health and care insurance
contract.                                                                             [½]
          Profitability                                                                                  [¼]
It will also be necessary to project the expected profit from the existing business, ...      [½]
... and its variance.                                                                          [¼]
          Supervisory reserves                                                                           [¼]
The actuary will need to come up with the assumptions to be used so that the reserves
and level of solvency capital required provide adequate security for policyholders.   [½]
          Investment                                                                                     [¼]
The actuary will need to provide input into how the insurance company should invest its
assets so as to maximise expected return within the resources available to it.        [½]
          Capital management                                                                             [¼]
Ultimately, the insurance company should be able to achieve its long‐term plans given the
resources available to it.                                                             [½]
         Risk management                                                                                [¼]
Consideration should be given to how underwriting and reinsurance can be used to
manage risk so that the insurance company can increase profits, while keeping its risk
profile within the resources available.                                                [½]
         Claims                                                                                         [¼]
The actuary should consider whether:          the claims functions are being properly followed                                     [½]
         there are effective fraud control measures in place.                          [½]

25.2.label: Question 25.2
25.2.scenario:
A proprietary health and care insurance company transacting critical illness insurance business is

reviewing its premium rates.

The revised premium rates are not competitive.
25.2.question:
Discuss possible reasons for this and the courses of action open to the insurance company if it
wishes to offer more competitive premium rates.

Solution: Size of the insurer – Larger insurers will generally have larger reserves and a more diversified
portfolio of business and hence will be less exposed to random fluctuation of claims experience.
The main reason is the risk of reinsurer default. If the reinsurer was unable to pay its claims then
the insurance company would still be liable to pay the original claims to its policyholders.
Therefore, even if an insurance company reinsured all of its business, it would still need to hold
some reserves in case things went wrong with the reinsurer(s).
Surplus treaty or excess of loss reinsurance might be used here (and other forms of
reinsurance might also be appropriate depending on the circumstances).
In fact, quota share is a very easy and effective way of reducing the solvency capital requirements.
Surplus treaty, excess of loss and quota share reinsurance will be described later in this chapter
(although you may already be familiar with them from an earlier subject).
1.6   Financial assistance (new business strain, improving free assets, merger /
acquisition)
Diversification – Since the insurer holds a smaller portion of each policy, it can write more policies
and hence achieve greater diversification.
Benefit from cheap reinsurance rates – An insurer will be more likely to purchase reinsurance if it
thinks it may be good value for money. This might occur at a time that competition in the
reinsurance market has been driving down prices.
Improve standing in the market – An insurer may want reinsurance in order to demonstrate to
other parties, such as rating agencies, that it is managing its risks appropriately.
Tax or solvency arbitrage – It may be possible to take advantage of being taxed on a different
basis from the reinsurer, or from having a different regulatory solvency capital requirement.
The insurer holds assets equal to the size of the reserves at any time. So when a claim occurs, the
insurer only has to find additional money to the extent that the sum insured exceeds the reserve.
(If the sum insured was equal to the reserve value at the date of claim, it wouldn’t matter to the
insurer how many claims occurred, as it would always have the money available to pay them.)
Hence, the insurer is only at risk from poor claims experience with the respect to this ‘sum at risk’.
If the insurer were to reinsure a proportion of the reserve as well as the sum at risk, then it would
be passing additional premiums to the reinsurer for no useful purpose. Given that using
reinsurance will reduce the insurer’s expected profit, this is a waste of money.
As for other proportional reinsurance types, sum‐at‐risk reinsurance can work either using a
quota share or a surplus arrangement.
(i)       Premiums received by the insurer and the reinsurer
The first split of the premium is $5,000 to each of the insurer and the quota share reinsurer. But
the insurer pays commission of $1,500 (ie 15% of $10,000) and receives $1,000 (ie 20% of $5,000)
from the reinsurer.
This leaves the net of commission premiums as $4,500 for the insurer and $4,000 for the quota
share reinsurer.
This split doesn’t look unfair. (You would expect the insurer to have a bigger share to compensate
for the initial work.)
(ii)      Reinsurance recovery
The insurer would be able to reclaim $25,000 from the reinsurer on a claim of $50,000.
This is a fairly simple case, however it is also possible that the insurer might want to limit the size
of the claims it will pay out to some maximum level. In this case, quota share would work as
above for sums insured that do not exceed the insurer’s maximum retention level. However, for
sums insured that do exceed this limit, the maximum limit would apply.
(i)         Reinsurance sum insured
No of           Sum insured     Insurer (£’000)     Reinsurer        Reinsurance sum insured
The insurer retains 40% of each sum insured, but this is restricted to a maximum of £100,000 per
policy in the case of the last two groups.
(i)       Problem with reinsurance programme
There is a ‘hole’ in the cover between £500,000 and £700,000. The layers of reinsurance ought
generally to be arranged so that there are no holes.
(ii)      Recoveries
Claim                1st layer     2nd layer        3rd layer         Total
Claim of 350,000 (when index = 105) – When the index is 105, the layers of reinsurance cover
become 105,000 in excess of 105,000 and 315,000 in excess of 210,000. Therefore the:
If it were not, then after taking out the cover the insurer could write a lot more business, and
trigger the stop loss limits that way. However, by using claim ratios, the limits (and the premium
charged for the cover) rise in proportion to the amount of business written by the insurer.
The lower limit is 1.05  292  306.6 and the upper limit is 1.25  292  365 .
(i)       Why reinsurers are often not prepared to offer stop loss cover
The contingent repayments will be higher, because not all of them may be made.
(i)       Why there would be little change to realistic embedded value
The assets would be increased by the amount of the loan, which would increase the embedded
value. On the other hand, the expected present value of the profits will be reduced on account of
the loan repayments. The two would be expected to be broadly equivalent in value, so that
overall there should be little change.
Looking slightly more deeply, the loan repayments will include a profit margin for the reinsurer,
which should reduce the expected future profits from the portfolio for the insurer. On the other
hand, the value of the future repayments is reduced in the embedded value calculation by
discounting them at the risk discount rate, realistically reflecting the possibility that that they will
not be paid. Once again, this points to the exchange of cash for profits to be broadly consistent,
especially if everything was valued in a market‐consistent way.
(ii)      How the insurer’s risk is expected to have changed
Without this reinsurance there is a risk that the VIF profit, which is included in the embedded
value, would not materialise.
With this reinsurance, part of that profit has been converted into current cash, making it certain.
The insurer’s profits are therefore subject to less uncertainty than before, so there is less risk.
This second approach (ie the contingent loan) may also be used where an insurer needs to
improve its solvency position, for example after a large drop in asset values, or where it
wishes to fund a new project, for example the setting up of a new subsidiary overseas.
(i)       The most important variable to model stochastically
This method will only be useful if the numbers of claims (eg sickness, critical illness rates) are
modelled stochastically.
(ii)      Differences for short‐term indemnity contracts
It would be necessary to model claim amounts as well as claim incidence rates stochastically.
Critical illness rates: Experience might be heavier than that of competitors, eg due to:                                         [½]
… or less sophisticated rating, eg no split by smoker status                                   [½]
… which may be due to selling through a different distribution channel                         [½]
… eg more CIs covered or weaker claim definitions.                                             [½]
So, the insurance company could: … or rating systems, eg introducing differential rates for smokers and non‐smokers to
         change target market and/or distribution channel                                               [½]
Alternatively or in addition, the insurance company could take a less conservative view in setting
the critical illness assumption, if this can be justified by evidence, …                         [½]
… eg from own data, industry or reinsurers, …                                                            [½]
… or by offering cover on a reviewable basis.                                                            [½]
Expenses: Expenses may be higher than those of competitors.                                                        [½]
The insurance company could look to reduce commission (if it is paying above market rates).        [½]
It could reduce sales costs by using more efficient marketing and increasing salesforce
… or try to improve administrative efficiency and reduce overheads.                                [½]
It may be able to achieve a lower price if total profits are improved by increased volumes.        [½]
Lapses: Lapse experience may be worse (heavier) than that of competitors.                                  [½]
It could try to improve persistency through improving sales methods and training of sales staff.
Lapse rates may differ due to different target market and/or distribution channel, and so it could
Profit target: The profit requirement could be too high relative to the market, so the insurance company could
reprice at reduced level of target profitability.                                            [½]
It should consider how much contribution is required from this business to overheads; …            [½]
… reducing the required contribution will reduce price.                                       [½]

Syllabus objective: 3.2       Demonstrate the application of reinsurance as a risk management technique.
## 3.2.1 Describe the purposes of re: ## 3.2.2     Describe the different types and structures of reinsurance
3.2.3     Discuss the factors that should be considered in determining the level of
0 Introduction: Reinsurance is insurance bought by an insurance company. The insurance company (also known
as the primary insurer, direct writer or cedant) passes on some of its risks to another party  the
There are several different ways an insurance company can pass on risks to a reinsurer, and there
are many different reasons for doing so. This chapter first considers the main reasons why
insurance companies might wish to use reinsurance and then goes on to describe the different
types of reinsurance and the reasons for using each.
Reinsurance language: There is quite a lot of specialist language in reinsurance. Although you will soon get used to it,
you will find it useful to be familiar with the following terms immediately:
         an insurer wishing to place some of its business with a reinsurer is normally termed
–         the term ‘cede’ means to ‘pass on’ or ‘give away’, as in ‘cede some risk to a
–         the term ‘cession’ refers to risks that have been ceded
         the term ‘facultative’ means ‘individual’, as in an individually negotiated arrangement
         a ‘treaty’ applies to a group of policies; this is reinsurance that the reinsurer is obliged to
accept, subject to conditions set out in the treaty.
1 Reasons for reinsurance: Some of the specific types of reinsurance introduced later in this chapter are referred to in this
section. You may find it useful to come back and re‐read this section after you have completed
Health and care insurance payouts will e:          be linked to a sum insured (for example, under a CI policy),
         be linked to a sum insured with agreed uplift (some mechanism for automatic
increasing of premium and/or benefit), or
         indemnify the policyholder against medical costs (for example, under a PMI policy).
In all cases, even the indemnity scenario, the insurer will be reasonably aware of the
potential maximum claim that each policy might produce.
Even under PMI policies, there are usually limits to the indemnity provided. In cases when there
are not, the insurer can estimate a likely maximum cost of treatment based on past experience.
The insurer will compare this to the available free assets to assess the approach by which,
and the extent to which, potential large risks should be transferred to reinsurers. Key
influences will be the insurer’s attitude to risk and the value for money afforded by the
For example, if the insurer has few free assets, is risk averse, and reinsurance is good value for
money, then it may decide to have an extensive reinsurance programme in place.
1.1 Limitation of exposure to risk: An insurer with a portfolio of business faces an uncertain future: despite the best advanced
statistical analysis and the utilisation of experienced underwriters, the claims outgo from
any period of risk cannot be predicted to any degree of accuracy. That is, after all, the point
of an insurance company: to cover the unknown financial consequences of possible future
perils affecting members of the public.
The factors that will determine the insurer’s appetite for offsetting its risk by using
\uf0b7 the size of its portfolio:          the degree to which it is felt that the business outcome is predictable within bounds.
Question: Describe briefly how these factors are likely to affect the amount of reinsurance an insurer uses.
Other than the six main reasons described in the Core Reading, can you think of any more reasons
for using reinsurance?
Explain the rationale for reinsuring only a proportion of the sum at risk in some cases as opposed
to reinsuring a proportion of the whole sum insured.
A health insurer has a 50% original terms quota share reinsurance treaty.
For a risk with a gross premium of $10,000, the cedant pays commission of 15% and receives 20%
commission from the reinsurer.
(i)       Calculate how much premium is received by the insurer and the reinsurer after allowing
for commissions. Do these relative levels of premium look fair?
(ii)      If there is subsequently a claim for $50,000, how much will the insurer be able to reclaim
from the reinsurer?
Why in practice would the insurer be keen to handle claims above the excess point prudently,
even if 100% of the claim above the excess was being paid by the reinsurer?
An insurer has two layers of risk XL cover. The first is 100,000 in excess of 100,000, the second is
300,000 in excess of 200,000. All limits are indexed, and the chosen index starts the treaty at a
value of 100.
Two separate claims are made: one of 350,000 when the index was 105 and the other of 600,000
when the index was 110.
Assuming that the treaties cover these claims, calculate how much each insurer pays for each.
Why is stop loss cover quoted in terms of claim ratios rather than monetary amounts?
(i)       Explain why reinsurers are often not prepared to provide stop loss cover.
(ii)      If the reinsurer does provide stop loss cover, what conditions is it likely to impose on the
business covered?
Would you expect the contingent loan repayments to be higher or lower in amount than
equivalent non‐contingent repayments, and why?
(i)       Explain why you would expect little change to the realistic embedded value of the insurer
if this type of financial reinsurance is used.
(ii)      Explain how you would expect the insurer’s risk to have changed as a result of this
reinsurance, with respect to the insurer’s realistic embedded value.
(i)       For long‐term health insurance contracts with fixed benefits (eg IP, CI), which is the most
important variable that needs to be modelled stochastically in this process?
(ii)      How would your answer to (i) change for short‐term contracts with indemnity benefits
As a result they may require less reinsu: However, it could be argued that larger insurers are more likely to write types of business that
‘need’ reinsurance – ie those with large and/or unusual risks. Also, large insurers may be more
prone to accumulations.
Experience in the marketplace – An insurer with a lot of experience in the marketplace will
hopefully have sufficient credible data to be able to make an objective estimate of the expected
claims outgo when setting premium rates and reserves. It should also be able to set sensible
policy conditions to help control the risk. As a result, it will be less likely to use reinsurance.
Amount of free assets – The larger an insurer’s available free assets, the bigger cushion it has to
absorb claims costs that are higher than expected, so the less the need for reinsurance.
Size of the portfolio – The larger the portfolio, the more credible the past claims experience, which
should better enable the insurer to predict future claims, and the more diversified the business,
which should lead to smoother results, both of which reduce the need for reinsurance.
Predictability of business outcome – Similarly, the more predictable the business outcome, the
less the need for reinsurance.
Note that these factors should not be considered in isolation; there are many factors affecting the
amount and type of reinsurance used and they should all be considered together. An overriding
factor in one case may be a minor factor in a different case, for example, an insurer with relatively
large free assets may use more reinsurance than an insurer with relatively low free assets because
it writes business with very volatile claims experience.
This is set against the availability of reinsurance. Some insurers may have an approach to
underwriting and claims management that means that some reinsurers are reluctant to offer
cover.
For example, reinsurers may generally not be willing to offer cover to a brand new, inexperienced
insurer writing CI cover without stringent underwriting.
Just like insurance companies, reinsurers are in the business to make money, usually for their
shareholders. They generally do not wish to write loss‐making business. If there is great
uncertainty about the future claims cost, then reinsurers may be inclined not to offer reinsurance
cover or at least charge much higher premiums than had previously been the case.
\uf0b7 expects to obtain higher:          expects to make profits from other connected business (eg from the same cedant, or
         is trying to retain market share, eg when the market is at the bottom of the (re)insurance
cycle, so that premiums across the whole market are low. In this case, reinsurers will
hope that premiums will soon increase making the business profitable once more.
Generally, where an insurer has fears of a claim or combination of claims that would
materially (negatively) affect the shape of results within that business line, reinsurance will
be considered. This may occur due to a single event (eg outbreak of insured disease),
and/or a concentration of risk in a territory.
1.2 Avoidance of large single losses: What is large to an insurer will depend on the size of the free assets available. Many small
to medium sized insurance companies will cede a top slice of potentially large payouts to
reinsurers, to ensure, as far as possible, that claims payments can be made without
detrimental effect to business results and, at the extreme, solvency.
An example of this is in respect of PMI cover. These claims can be very large, at least in some
territories, running to hundreds of thousands of pounds, euro, dollars etc. Insurers may want to
limit their exposure to such claims to help control the impact on the free assets or profits.
1.3 Smoothing of results: The principle whereby reinsurance covers the larger risks or accumulation of smaller risks
above certain limits helps to achieve a smooth development of accounts year-on-year,
especially when the portfolio is relatively immature.
A premium is paid to mitigate these fluctuations and the net result is more predictable for
the insurer, a predictability that may also be more acceptable to shareholders and
regulators. Reinsurance thus helps to reduce the variance of the insurer’s expected
disability or mortality experience relative to the mean.
Stop loss is a form of reinsurance that: Stop loss will be described in more detail later in this chapter, but as its name suggests it reduces
extreme losses directly and hence helps to smooth profits. However, stop loss isn’t the only type
of reinsurance that will smooth results. Nearly all types of reinsurance will smooth profits to
some degree. This might be achieved by simply limiting claim amounts (as mentioned above) or
by enabling the insurer to write a larger number of smaller risks and hence achieve a greater
diversification of risk, which will have lower variability.
1.4   Availability of expertise (new risks, unusual risks, new territories)
When an insurer is adopting a strategy that will take it into new risk areas where it has little
previous experience, the reinsurer can help with product design, rating / pricing,
underwriting and claims management. Often in these circumstances, the method of
coverage will be quota share, possibly with a low initial retention. Once the insurer’s
expertise and confidence has grown, the reinsurer’s involvement will reduce.
Quota share will also be described in more detail later in this chapter but for now it’s sufficient to
know that it’s a relatively simple type of reinsurance. In the situation described here it can be
initially set up to pass most of the risk to the reinsurer so that the insurer retains only a small
amount of risk. Over a number of years, the insurer is likely to increase the amount of risk it
retains and hence the amount of reinsurance used will decrease as it grows more confident of its
1.5 Increasing capacity to accept risk: Owing to insufficient capital backing, an insurer may be reluctant to accept, or incapable of
accepting, particular risks by sector or by volume.
An insurer may also be reluctant to accept a particular risk if it would be exposed to an
accumulation of risk as a result.
Reinsurance cover can assist in this situation. The solvency (capital) requirements for a line
of business are normally reduced in line with the proportion ceded, though this may be
Solvency capital requirements were discussed in Chapter 22. Using reinsurance can usually
reduce the solvency capital an insurance company needs to hold, however, this reduction may be
subject to an upper limit. In other words, you can only reduce the solvency capital requirement
so far and there may be a minimum level that the insurance company still needs to hold.
New business strain: Across both long-term and, to a lesser extent, short-term health insurance lines,
reinsurance funds are available to assist financially with particular business propositions.
Where a particular distribution strategy would involve substantially more cash outflow in
the initial stages than premium income, reinsurance commission may be available to ‘factor’
future surplus streams, ie lend now against the predicted future flows of premiums less
Here, the reinsurer is paying some money to the insurer to improve its cash balance, often to
finance new business strain, but alternatively it could be for other purposes, eg setting up a new
distribution channel and/or computer system. This payment is effectively a loan, but is ‘disguised’
as a commission payment for the reinsurance. In return, the insurer will repay the loan out of the
profits it makes on the underlying business.
Improving free assets: Occasionally, where the reinsurer agrees that a block of renewing business should produce
regular profits in the future, it may be possible for the reinsurer to provide capital to the
insurer to improve its free asset position by reinsuring this portfolio of profitable existing
business. The reinsurer would pay an initial commission, after which the reinsurer would
be entitled to (a proportion of) the future surpluses arising on this portfolio for as long as
This type of arrangement may be more attractive to the insurer than, say, a simple bank loan
because it has no liability to repay the loan unless a surplus has been made, and so the insurer
does not have to reserve for the future payments. (In accounting terms, the arrangement is ‘off
balance sheet’.) It has therefore increased its assets by the amount of the loan but not increased
its liabilities, and hence has improved its free asset position.
However, as described in Section 7, whether there is any balance sheet benefit of such an
arrangement depends on the regulatory reporting environment.
The scope for such arrangements has been significantly reduced under certain regimes, such as
Solvency II, which governs insurance companies operating in EU countries.
Merger / acquisition: A similar exercise might facilitate the acquisition of an insurance company, where a subset
of business (or all policies) within the company being acquired is identified as potentially
profitable, and the reinsurer is prepared to advance funds in anticipation of this future
Here, the buying insurer has the reinsurance in order to give it a cash advance, which will help it
So, this is similar to the financing of new business strain described above.
In each of these cases quota share reinsurance is the norm, though any form of
Other types of reinsurance may be used to improve the free asset position that involve little or no
risk transfer from the insurer to the reinsurer. Such contracts are referred to as financial
reinsurance and are described later in the chapter.
Solvency capital arbitrage: This occurs when the reinsurer does not have to hold as much capital – eg as supervisory solvency
capital – for a given unit of risk as the insurer. In this case, the cost of the capital required to
manage the business will be less in the reinsurer’s hands, and part of that saving can be
transferred to the insurer by offering attractive (ie cheap) reinsurance premium rates.
(Alternatively some other compensation, such as higher commission, could be paid.) It should
then be possible to pitch the reinsurer’s premium rate at a level that will increase the insurer’s
profits as well as those of the reinsurer.
Tax arbitrage: In a similar way, tax arbitrage might be possible when the reinsurer is subject to less onerous
taxes than the insurer so that reinsurance can be provided on relatively favourable rates.
2 Types of reinsurance business: There are many variations of the basic types of reinsurance, and contracts are often tailored
to meet the particular needs of the cedant and reinsurer concerned. However, all
reinsurance contracts are based on the same underlying principles.
As a general rule in health and care insurance, reinsurance tends to be arranged on a treaty,
rather than a facultative, basis. For long-term products, the treaties tend to be of a
proportional nature (either surplus or quota share). The short-term contracts, when
reinsured, may be reinsured in different ways, proportional (quota share) and non-
proportional (eg excess of loss). All of these terms are explained in the following sections.
Financial: These types are not mutually exclusive  there is some overlap between them, as you will see.
3 Reinsurance commission: Reinsurance commission is used to describe a payment from the reinsurer to the insurer.
Reinsurance commission is typically paid under proportional reinsurance business written on an
Sometimes it is structured as a deduction from the premium paid to the reinsurer, in which
case it would typically be called a rebate.
In the following sections the term commission can also be read to mean a rebate.
4 Facultative and treaty reinsurance: Insurers cede risks either facultatively or by treaty according to how they weigh up the
advantages and disadvantages of each basis.
4.1 Facultative: The term ‘facultative’ applied to the cedant’s part of the agreement means that it is free to
place the reinsurance with any reinsurer. Similarly, so far as the reinsurer is concerned,
facultative means that it may accept or reject the reinsurance as offered.
The main advantage of facultative reinsurance is the flexibility that both parties have within the
process. For example, the cedant is under no obligation to use a particular reinsurer. The cedant
can approach several reinsurers in search of the best terms for each risk individually. Similarly,
the reinsurer is under no obligation to accept risks.
The main disadvantages to the insurer of:          it is a time‐consuming and costly exercise to place such risks
         there is no certainty that the required cover will be available when needed
         even if cover is available, the price and terms may be unacceptable
         the insurer may be unable to accept a large risk until it has been able to find the required
reinsurance cover. This means the insurer cannot accept business automatically when it
is offered, and consequently its standing in the market may be reduced.
Facultative cessions of long-term insurance risks generally concern individual proposals
that are outside the normal range of policies accepted, eg unusually large or with unusual
4.2 Treaty: The term ‘obligatory’ indicates the removal of this freedom of action. The agreement
between cedant and reinsurer may be:
Obligatory / Obligatory: Here, the first word refers to the situation from the insurer’s perspective and the second word
refers to the reinsurer’s position. Thus ‘facultative / obligatory’ means that the insurer can
choose whether or not to reinsure the risk, while the reinsurer is obliged to accept the risk if the
insurer decides to cede it.
Insurers tend to place the bulk of reinsurance cover on a treaty basis.
Arranging reinsurance for each individual risk is administratively messy. Therefore insurers may
instead set up treaties with reinsurers. This allows them to place reinsurance automatically. The
terms and conditions of the treaty are carefully laid down so that both parties know exactly where
they stand. A treaty overcomes the disadvantages of facultative reinsurance listed above.
Arrangements that are obligatory to the reinsurer might seem potentially risky to it. However,
the treaty will only apply to certain specified products, and will only have been agreed when the
reinsurer is satisfied about the cedant’s underwriting procedures. It may be that the cedant is
taking on ‘bad’ risks – for example, CI policies sold via direct marketing with no medical
underwriting. This is not a problem for the reinsurer if it is aware of this and can therefore charge
appropriate reinsurance premiums, or pay appropriately low reinsurance commission in the case
of original terms reinsurance.
Even if the cedant manages to select against the reinsurer, so that the reinsurer’s experience is
much worse than the cedant’s experience, the reinsurer is likely to increase premiums or change
the terms of the treaty at the next renewal date of the treaty. The reinsurer may even decide not
to reinsure the cedant again.
An insurer will also wish to use reinsurance to control its solvency and growth
requirements. This can be done more readily by treaty arrangements.
Treaties are usually arranged so that the cedant is obliged under the terms of the treaty to
pass on some of the risk in a defined manner and the reinsurer is obliged to accept it.
The features of treaties are very much t: Inflexible:           Once the treaty is set up, then both parties must operate within the terms of the
treaty (so each should be happy with the terms before they sign the treaty).
Efficient:            Risks are generally reinsured automatically. This is administratively quicker and
Certain:              With a treaty, the cedant knows that reinsurance is available (if the risk falls
within the limits of the treaty) and on what terms.
Terms within the treaty: It is essential for both the insurer and the reinsurer that the treaty is absolutely precise in its
\uf0b7 what is and what is not:           the financial arrangements (ie premiums, commissions, timing of payments)
\uf0b7 the obligations of both: The treaty document sets out all the relevant details and obligations under the arrangement,
though the wording differs for different types of reinsurance. There will also be wide
differences in wording between treaties in any one category.
5.1 Introduction: The first type of reinsurance is proportional, whereby the insurer cedes a proportion of the risk
and the reinsurer pays that proportion of the total sum insured or sum at risk.
\uf0b7 surplus: either of which can be on:
\uf0b7 an original terms (coinsu:          a risk premium basis (related either to the full benefit or to the sum‐at‐risk) .
\uf0b7 quota share (on an origin: Under proportional reinsurance, the reinsurer covers an agreed proportion of each risk.
This proportion may be constant for all risks covered (ie quota share) or will relate the
insurer’s preferred monetary retention to the overall size of sum insured (ie surplus, under
Both forms have to be administered automatically, and therefore require a treaty.
These types of proportional reinsurance are discussed in more detail below.
Purposes of proportional reinsurance: Proportional reinsurance reduces the size of the cedant’s net account, and so it is used
mostly as a means of accepting a larger size of risk than would otherwise be possible.
Proportional reinsurance is particularly popular with new or inexperienced cedants that are not
comfortable with the uncertainty and possible volatility of a new product or area, and thus would
like to share the experience with another party (the reinsurer).
A proportional reinsurance arrangement allows reinsurance commissions to be payable,
whereby the insurer’s new business costs or other appetite for revenue is satisfied by
‘factoring’ the future margins in premiums to be passed to the reinsurer.
Future premiums should be more than sufficient to cover all the insurer’s outgo, so that it makes
some profit. Under a proportional reinsurance arrangement, the reinsurer will therefore take a
share of this profit, however, since it has not suffered the expense of selling the original business,
its profits will be relatively higher than the insurer’s.
Therefore it is only fair that the reinsurer ‘pays’ for this benefit, by paying the insurer a
commission to cover its share of the expenses. The commission terms will be stipulated in the
Changes in the proportion used: In certain circumstances, the reinsurance will diminish over time, as the insurer gains
experience of the new product or the new territory. Thus, the treaty will incorporate an
increasing monetary retention (surplus) or a reducing proportional share (quota share).
By increasing the insurer’s retention or reducing the proportional share that the reinsurer takes,
the insurer will keep more of the risk for itself.
These lower cessions would normally only apply to new business, ie the proportions set for
a policy at its inception remain unchanged until the policy expires. However, on agreement
between the two parties, changes in proportion can apply also to existing business.
5.2 Original terms (coinsurance) reins: [In the UK and Ireland this method is usually referred to as ‘original terms’ reinsurance,
whereas in most other countries it is known as ‘coinsurance’.]
Original terms reinsurance can be applied to either quota share or surplus, both of which are
This method involves a sharing of all aspects of the original contract. It applies to both
Hence the premium is split between the insurer and reinsurer in fixed proportions and any claim
is split in the same proportion. This will generally mean that the reinsurer shares in full the risks
of the policy, including the risks of investment and early lapse.
The cedant will provide the reinsurer with the premium rates it is using for the particular
class of business it wishes to reinsure.
This is so that the reinsurer can check that the cedant’s premium rates are adequate.
Commission: The reinsurer will determine the rates of reinsurance commission it is prepared to pay to the
cedant for the business. In doing so, it will have regard to its likely future experience and
its knowledge of the quality of the cedant’s underwriting.
Reinsurance commission is not relevant to excess of loss reinsurance, although profit commission
is possible. (However, profit commission would be totally inappropriate for the very high layer
excess of loss treaties, where the reinsurer expects to pay out only in exceptional circumstances.)
It will also want to make a profit on th: In addition, the reinsurance commission will usually cover – in respect of the reinsured
portion of the policy – the commission that has been paid by the cedant and part or all of
Deposits back: In certain countries, the supervisory authority may require the reinsurer to ‘deposit back’ its
share of the total reserve under a reinsured contract with the cedant. Even where it is not a
requirement, this is sometimes done so that the cedant gets the benefit of reinsurance
whilst at the same time being able to maintain a reserve for the whole contract and hence
Each year the reinsurer would have to give the insurer enough money to enable the insurer to set
up the full statutory reserve for the policy.
Any investment profits earned on the statutory reserve then accrue entirely to the insurer. At the
same time, the reinsurer takes none of the investment risk under the contract.
The overall result is that the reinsurer only in effect covers the (reinsured part of) the sum insured
over and above the reserve (called the sum at risk). It makes the arrangement generally more
profitable to the insurer, as it will retain all of its potential investment profit.
However, the arrangement can also be an advantage to the reinsurer. For example, in the case of
a large international reinsurer, it may avoid any problems with having to invest in an unfamiliar
The deposit back arrangement will also serve to mitigate the reinsurer default risk to which
5.3 Risk premium reinsurance: In the case of original terms reinsurance, the insurer sets its premiums and passes on an
appropriate amount to the reinsurer and then negotiates an amount of commission to receive
In contrast, in the case of risk premium reinsurance, the reinsurer sets its premium rate, which is
independent of the premium charged by the insurer.
With a risk premium structure, changes in the cedant’s premium rates will not necessarily
require changes in reinsurance rates. It therefore gives the cedant greater freedom to
respond to competitor changes in premium rates.
Types of risk premium reinsurance: Here, the reinsurer does not share in the premium received by the cedant, but charges a
specific premium for the risk, which may be level over the term of the policy or may vary
annually with the probability of claim.
Recall that risk premium reinsurance is used for long‐term health and care insurance products, so
the insurer will need to pay the reinsurer (annual) premiums over the term of the insurance
\uf0b7 increasing over the term: The premium rates as determined above are applied to the sum reinsured, which may be a
proportion of the sum insured or of the sum at risk.
Level risk premiums, increasing risk premiums and sum‐at‐risk reinsurance are described next.
Level risk premiums: Under a ‘net level premium arrangement’, the reinsurer spreads the risk premiums so that
they are level over the term of the contract. These may be guaranteed or not, often in line
with the cedant’s own product approach. This method has the advantage that the cedant, if
desired, can simply load the reinsurance charges to obtain the premium payable by the
The reinsurer is here charging a level premium for the benefit it is reinsuring, in much the same
way as the insurer might charge a level premium for an insurance contract with an individual
policyholder. The premiums may be guaranteed, or they may be reviewable at regular intervals.
In the latter case, when the premiums are reviewed, they are then set to be level over the
Increasing yearly risk premiums: Here each year’s risk premium represents the expected cost of the claims payable by the
reinsurer during the year. As policyholders get older over the term of their insurance contracts,
the risk of making claims also increases – hence the reinsurer’s risk premiums increase.
It may or may not guarantee these rates: So the reinsurer’s risk premium rates – be they level or yearly increasing – could be guaranteed or
reviewable, in much the same way as the insurer’s premium rates can be.
Sum\u2010at\u2010risk reinsurance: A variant of (risk premium) proportional reinsurance is the concept of ‘sum at risk’
reinsurance on long-term contracts. Here the proportions are applied, not to the whole sum
insured, but to the insurer’s sum at risk, ie the excess of the stated policy benefit over the
reserve that the cedant holds.
The sum‐at‐risk method is only of use where the benefit is a lump sum, terminating the contract,
and the reserves are large enough to make the adjustment significant. While commonly used for
several kinds of life insurance contract, this approach is less usual for health and care insurance:
         IP insurance and LTCI typically pay regular benefits rather than a lump sum, and as the
claim duration is unknown in advance, the overall benefit will be unknown; determination
of the sum at risk is therefore not possible, so it is necessary to reinsure (a proportion of)
the full benefit amount
         CI insurance provides a terminal lump sum, however, reserves are typically small relative
to the size of the benefit – in these cases, the reinsurance premiums based on full‐benefit
or sum‐at‐risk approaches differ little from each other, so it is not worth the
administrative hassle of using the more complicated sum‐at‐risk method.
A possible exception (where the sum‐at‐risk method might be appropriate) is for unit‐linked CI.
For ease of administration, the terms of the treaty may stipulate the amounts of reserves
from the outset, or the basis on which they are calculated.
For unit-linked products, the sum at risk will be the excess of benefit over the bid value of
units. (This process may be further simplified by holding the unit value notionally constant
at its value at the start of the year, for reinsurance calculation purposes.)
Calculating risk premiums: The reinsurer determines its risk premium rates by assessing the likely experience of the
business that it is to reinsure and then adding expense and profit margins.
There may additionally be some form of profit participation, whereby a share of any profits to the
reinsurer from the portfolio reinsured is paid back to the insurer.
5.4 Quota share: Quota share is a type of proportional reinsurance and is always by treaty. It is a simple
mechanism for ceding a portion of the business. It can be used for long‐term business (either on
an original terms or risk premium basis, as already discussed), or for short‐term business (on an
Suppose the insurer decides that for a particular class it does not want to retain all the business it
writes. So the insurer enters into a quota share treaty with a reinsurer and a constant proportion
of each and every risk within the scope of the treaty is automatically passed to the reinsurer. The
treaty will specify the proportion to be ceded to the reinsurer, R% say. This is often referred to as
Premiums: The premiums can either be calculated on a risk premium or original terms basis.
Claims: The reinsurer pays the insurer R% of the claims from risks covered by the treaty.
The insurer has purchased a 60% quota share reinsurance treaty with a maximum retention of

Practical considerations: The administration of quota share is very straightforward, especially if it is done on an original
In this case, because every risk is automatically reinsured, there is no need for anything more than
a list of all the risks with total premiums and total claims. The premiums and claims payable
between the cedant and the reinsurer (ignoring commission) are a straight proportion of the
Risk premium quota share will necessarily be more complicated. This is because the reinsurer’s
premium rates need to be applied the reinsured benefit amounts (eg to R% of the full value of the
benefits or to R% of the sum at risk), in order to obtain the reinsurance premiums payable (as
Reinsurer\u2019s claims experience: The cedant and reinsurer will have proportionately the same overall underwriting
experience on the business included in the treaty, apart from differences in expenses and
commissions. The reinsurer will therefore be concerned at the outset to establish:
\uf0b7 any previous experience o: The level of commission offered by the reinsurer will reflect the reinsurer’s view of the
expected claims. Generally, the reinsurer will reserve the right via the treaty to be involved
in the approval and settlement process for claims above a certain size. The reinsurer might
also negotiate for part of the commission payment to the cedant to be a profit commission,
payable only if the business ceded meets specified profitability criteria.
Remember, however, that reinsurance commission is only usually payable when the reinsurance
is on an original terms basis.
The treaty may contain some portfolio vo: This is so that the capacity of the reinsurer to write such business is limited. This limit will
normally be expressed in terms of the original gross premium income of the cedant for that
Purposes of quota share: Quota share is widely used by insurers to spread risk, write larger portfolios of risk and
encourage reciprocal business. It also directly improves the solvency ratio and helps the
insurer to satisfy the statutory solvency (capital) requirement.
In addition, it might help to finance new business strain (or other activities).
Spreading risk: A large, established insurer will be far less dependent (if at all) on quota share as a means
of spreading risk than a small, new insurer. However, large insurers often employ quota
share extensively where there is a launch of a product covering an area of risk where the
insurer is inexperienced (eg critical illness) or a launch of an existing product into a new
Here, reinsurance is being used to reduce the insurer’s exposure to losses arising from mis‐pricing
the product – ie parameter risk. The greater uncertainty the insurer has regarding the claims
experience assumptions in its premium basis, the greater proportion it will wish to reinsure.
Writing larger portfolios of risk: Another aspect of spreading the risk that can be achieved with quota share is that, for the same
total (net‐of‐reinsurance) benefits at risk, the insurer will be able to insure more individual risks.
That is, an insurer’s risks are better spread by retaining 50% of 1,000 risks than 100% of 500 risks.
Encouraging reciprocal business: Quota share can also help spread risks through reciprocity. This is where two insurers agree to
swap some business on a reciprocating basis. For example, Insurer A may have a 40% quota share
treaty with Insurer B for business it writes directly, while Insurer B has a similar quota share treaty
with Insurer A. Provided the two portfolios are not perfectly correlated, both insurers will have
achieved a better spread of risks. An example of this would be two health insurers writing
different business (eg CI insurance and PMI) or business in different locations, thus spreading risk
to limit the impact of adverse experience in one or other of these products or territories.
Improving the solvency ratio: For short‐term business, the solvency ratio is often defined as free assets divided by net written
premiums. Using quota share will reduce net written premiums and hence increase the solvency
Satisfying the statutory solvency capita: Many statutory solvency capital requirements for short‐term insurance are based on net written
premiums. The legislation may require that the insurer has solvency capital at least as great as a
defined percentage of net written premiums. Quota share can be used directly to reduce this
Financing: Reinsurance commission paid under original terms quota share arrangements might be used to
help finance new business strain, developing new products, mergers / acquisitions etc.
\uf0b7 there might be some recip:          it can help to increase solvency ratios for short‐term business
         it can help to satisfy the statutory solvency capital requirement
\uf0b7 used for financing new bu: In addition, quota share has the advantage that it is administratively simple.
The main disadvantages to an insurer of:          it cedes the same proportion of each risk, irrespective of size; the insurer may,
however, wish to cede a greater proportion of the larger risks than the smaller ones,
owing to their greater loss potential
The Glossary contains the following defi: ‘Under a surplus reinsurance arrangement, the long-term insurer will cede to the reinsurer
all sums that exceed its retention on each individual life.’
This definition of surplus reinsurance applies to long‐term insurance, where the sum insured is
known. For short‐term insurances – where the benefit amount is usually not known – it is
necessary to use an estimated maximum loss (EML).
The definition above is relevant for Subject SP1 because in a health and care insurance context,
surplus reinsurance is only used for long‐term products.
This class of reinsurance cover will only apply to policies where there is a fixed sum
insured, chosen at the outset by the policyholder. Thus, it will not apply to private medical
The reason for this is that in order to split the risk into proportions (ie that ceded and that
retained), the overall size of the claim must be quantifiable.
Like quota share, surplus is a proportional form of reinsurance arranged under a treaty. The key
difference is that the proportion of risk ceded will vary from risk to risk, depending on the size of
the sum insured under the policy, in relation to the retention agreed under the treaty for the class
of business concerned. The same retention level will apply for all business covered by a particular
surplus treaty until that treaty is either amended or abolished.
Retention level: \u00a375,000: Policy 1:           Sum insured £150,000, so £75,000 (150,000  75,000) is reinsured, ie 50%.
Policy 2:           Sum insured £100,000, so £25,000 (100,000  75,000) is reinsured, ie 25%.
Policy 3:           Sum insured £50,000, so there is no reinsurance (as the sum insured is below the
Purpose of surplus insurance: Surplus cover enables an insurer to write larger risks, which might otherwise be beyond its
writing capacity. The main value of this type of reinsurance, however, is to enable the
insurer to limit its exposure for the policies concerned, ie the insurer selects a monetary
limit at the outset of the treaty and reinsures the amount of any policy sum insured above
The main difference between surplus and quota share is that for quota share, the same
percentage is used for every policyholder, regardless of the size of the sum insured. Surplus
reinsurance is therefore much more efficient at targeting the morbidity risk. To avoid high risk
concentration, and yet to maximise profits, the insurer would seek to reinsure a high proportion
of its very large risks, but none at all of its small cases. This is precisely what is achieved using the
surplus method, and this results in reduced claims volatility.
Both types of proportional reinsurance will reduce the insurer’s total exposure to parameter and
model risks in proportion to the total amount reinsured in either case.
Whether the insurer prefers surplus or a quota share arrangement, or some combination thereof,
will depend on the insurer’s need for each type of service.
Where the sum insured increases under the basic policy (in line with some inflationary
index or by a fixed percentage accumulation each year), the treaty will stipulate whether the
sum retained remains fixed in monetary terms or whether the insurer and reinsurer continue
to share the increases in line with the original proportion.
Advantages of surplus to an insurer are:          allows the insurer to accept risks that would otherwise be too big (more effectively than
         reduces concentration of risk per policy and so reduces claims volatility.
Disadvantages of surplus are that:          the insurer has less control of protection against parameter risk than under quota share,
as its overall share of the risk will be dependent on the sizes of the policies taken on
6 Non\u2010proportional reinsurance: Proportional reinsurance can be used to spread risk and to reduce pro rata the size of risk
retained. However, this does not cap the cost of very large claims that occasionally occur
under private medical insurance policies. Under excess of loss (XL) reinsurance, on the
other hand, the cost to an insurer of a large claim is capped with the liability above a certain
level (often called the lower limit, or retention) being passed to a reinsurer. However, if the
claim amount exceeds the upper limit of the reinsurance, the excess will revert to the
insurer. Variations of this form of reinsurance cover exist to limit an insurer’s loss from a
single event or over a given period.
With non\u2010proportional reinsurance:          The lower limit might operate on individual claims (eg to cover against large individual
risks), or on aggregations of claims (eg to cover against widespread disease).
         There might also be an upper limit, above which the reinsurer’s liability ends.
         The reinsurer might pay for all claims within the limits, or perhaps a proportion of the
\uf0b7 The limits might be linke: The various types of excess of loss reinsurance are covered in more detail later in this section.
Non-proportional insurance is unlikely to be found in long-term insurances where the size
of the benefit (sum insured) is known at the outset, though aggregate excess of loss may be
appropriate, eg for group IP covers.
The primary aim of non‐proportional reinsurance is that it covers the insurer in the event of
claims being larger than expected. Therefore, it would probably not be appropriate for individual
policies where the sum insured is fixed from the outset, because the claim size is already known.
However, on group contracts, the total claims could be greater than expected (ie more claims
might have occurred), therefore an aggregate XL reinsurance might be appropriate.
How excess of loss operates: In its simplest form, the reinsurer agrees to indemnify the cedant for the amount of any loss
above a stated excess point. More usually, the reinsurer will give cover up to a stated upper
limit, with the insurer purchasing further layers of XL cover, which stack on top of the
primary layer, from different reinsurers. The higher layer cover(s) come into operation on
any particular loss only when the lower layer cover has been fully used (or ‘burnt through’).
The layers of reinsurance should be arranged so that there are no gaps, ie the lower limit of the
second layer of XL reinsurance starts at the upper limit of the first XL reinsurance.
The top layer of XL reinsurance might be unlimited (ie there is no upper limit).
The expression generally used to describe the cover provided under an excess of loss reinsurance
Amount of layer in excess of lower limit: So, a treaty that provides cover for claim amounts between a lower limit (excess point) of £50,000
and an upper limit of £200,000 would be described as:
\uf0b7 \u00a32 million in excess: (i)       What is silly about this reinsurance programme?
(ii)      How much will the insurer be able to recover in respect each of the following claims?
Indexed limits: Where inflation has a significant effect on the cost of claims, a stability clause may be
applied to the excess point; this provides for the indexation of the monetary limit in line with
a specified inflation index. This is done to achieve a more equitable division between the
reinsurer and cedant of the inflationary element of claims covered under the treaty. The
cedant will normally be required to pay an extra premium to compensate the reinsurer for
the added risk if the excess point is not indexed.
The upper limit (where one exists) may similarly be indexed to preserve the original real
The basis for indexation should be a reliable inflation index that bears some relation to the
inflationary effects on the claim sizes.
Proportional within non\u2010proportiona: For all forms of XL, it is possible that the reinsurer will cover only a proportion of the claims within
For example, the cover might be set as 90% of £150,000 in excess of £50,000. In this case, a claim
of £120,000 would generate a recovery of £63,000 (ie 90% of £70,000).
The reason for this type of arrangement is to give the insurer more incentive to keep the claim
settlements low. Otherwise, the reinsurer may feel exposed to the ‘moral hazard’ of the insurer
having sloppy settlement procedures for large medical claims.
The insurer will want to handle claims p:          it will want to maintain a good long‐term relationship with the reinsurer
6.2 Risk excess of loss (Risk XL): This is a type of excess of loss reinsurance that relates to individual losses. It affects only
one insured risk at any one time. This is sometimes found under private medical insurance
portfolios where individual large losses sometimes occur.
The insurer pays a premium to the reinsurer in return for the protection.
\uf0b7 second XL reinsurer pays: Claim of 600,000 (when index = 110) – When the index is 110, the layers of reinsurance cover
become 110,000 in excess of 110,000 and 330,000 in excess of 220,000. Therefore the:
         insurer is left with the remaining 50,000, ie paying 160,000 in total.
6.3 Aggregate excess of loss (Aggregat: Aggregate XL / stop loss reinsurance is an extension of risk XL reinsurance. However, rather than
operating on large individual claims, the excess point and the upper limit apply to the aggregation
of multiple claims.
Sometimes a class of business gives rise to large variations in the levels of total claims
payable in any one year.
This may be due to one or more large individual claims (in which case risk XL can be used), but it
may also be due to a large number of small claims, perhaps resulting from a concentration of
risks. Individually, each claim might not be of exceptional size, but collectively, the aggregate cost
may be damaging to the insurer’s profitability. In this case, risk XL will fail to protect the insurer
adequately against the aggregate cost of such losses.
An insurer may therefore wish to protect the class of business by a form of reinsurance that
extends cover to all events during the year.
In other subjects (such as Subject CP1), you may have read that aggregate XL and stop loss are
two different types of non‐proportional reinsurance. The main difference between them is that
aggregate XL typically covers specified perils, whereas stop loss covers all perils. This makes sense
in the context of general insurance, where the insurer may wish to protect itself against specific
perils, such as fire or flood. However, in health insurance, the only peril (ie cause of claim) is
morbidity. Since any aggregate XL contract would cover this peril, it is effectively covering all
perils, and hence in a health insurance context, aggregate XL and stop loss can be considered to
be the same. Therefore, in Subject SP1 the terms aggregate XL and stop loss are used
interchangeably.
Stop loss protects the insurer by covering the total losses for the whole account, above an
agreed limit, for a 12-month period. The whole account can comprise one or several
classes of insurance.
The excess point (or attachment point) and upper limit (or exhaustion point) for stop loss
are often expressed as a percentage of the cedant’s premium income for that account.
Cover might typically be given from an excess point of 110% claims ratio up to an upper
limit of 130% or 140%.
The claims ratio is normally defined as total claims incurred divided by the earned premiums. The
calculation of earned premiums was covered in Chapter 21.

Reinsurers are often not prepared to pro:          the reinsurer has only limited control over initial underwriting and claim payments made
Conditions the reinsurer may impose befo:          impose a deductible so that insurer retains a proportion of the risk
\uf0b7 require some control over: Profits in long-term insurances are rarely sufficiently volatile to warrant paying for this
6.4 Catastrophe excess of loss (Catast: Alternatively, cover may be defined in terms of a common cause or peril (single event) over
Examples of the above might be:          a chemical explosion (a single event) giving rise to a number of claims which
together might exceed an insurer’s retention, though individually they would not
If the total amount of all claims arising from the single event exceeds the lower limit, then
the insurer can make a recovery from the reinsurer.
         a pandemic that might result in a considerable increase in the number of claims.
An insurer might face a regional epidemic, for example, policyholders living in a particular
area might collectively make a large number individual claims as a result of a particular
illness (eg influenza (‘flu’)), none of which in isolation is too worrying, but which in total
could damage the insurer.
In its most extreme (and far less likely) form, it may be of catastrophic proportions, eg a
country‐wide (or even continent‐ or world‐wide) illness, eg a virulent form of flu (such as
bird flu or swine flu). This may involve losses to many hundreds or even thousands of
different insured risks (again, all from the same cause) with a potential cost falling beyond
the normal capacity and intention of other aggregate treaties and possibly even beyond
the finances of a large insurer.
Note that in the event of a severe epidemic, the government(s) may be likely to step in –
either to support the affected individuals, or the insurance industry.
Event-type cover is generally available. However, the risks associated with pandemic cover
are very uncertain and so this cover is not normally available; at least not at an affordable
price.
This means that an insurer may be able to afford to purchase catastrophe XL reinsurance to cover
the risk of a chemical explosion event occurring, however, it is unlikely to be able to find
affordable cover for the risk of a pandemic occurring.
6.5 Purposes of non\u2010proportional: The main purpose of any form of excess of loss reinsurance is to permit an insurer to
accept risks that could lead to large claims. Large here means large relative to the insurer’s
Other purposes are:          to reduce the risk of insolvency from a catastrophe, a large claim or an aggregation
         to stabilise the technical results of the insurer by reducing claims fluctuations.
By reducing the variance of the claims outgo, the annual profitability of the insurer will be less
volatile, which will be preferable for shareholders.
With lower volatility of claims, the insurer can also make more efficient use of its capital. High
volatility of outgo means that the insurer must hold large reserves. If the volatility is reduced,
lower reserves are required. This means that the insurer can write the same amount of business
with less capital by using excess of loss reinsurance.
Advantages of individual excess of loss:          allows insurer to take on risks that could produce very large claims
\uf0b7 helps stabilise profits f:          helps make more efficient use of the capital by reducing the variance of the claim
On the downside, as with all reinsurance, the insurer will pay a premium to the reinsurer which, in
the long run, will be greater than the expected recoveries under the treaty. An excess of loss
reinsurer’s premium will load the expected claims for expenses, profit and contingency margins.
Also, from time to time, XL premiums may be considerably greater than the pure risk premium for
the cover. For example, after reinsurers have had a few years of poor results, the supply of
reinsurance falls and premiums rise as reinsurers attempt to restore their solvency positions.
7.1 Features of financial reinsurance: A wide variety of financial reinsurance contracts have been developed and used, although
all were devised primarily as a means of improving the apparent accounting or supervisory
One feature of a financial reinsurance contract is that it tends to involve only a small
element, if any, of transfer of insurance risk from the cedant to the reinsurer.
Rather than transferring insurance risk (which is the aim of traditional reinsurance), financial
reinsurance aims to manage the insurer’s capital position. The methods that can be used to
achieve this are outlined in the next two sections.
Financial reinsurance is not effective under accounting or supervisory regimes where credit
can already be taken for the future profits and/or where a realistic liability must be held in
respect of the loan repayments. With the increased global movement towards realistic
reporting bases, there is likely to be reduced adoption of such arrangements. Refer back to
Chapter 13 for a reminder of the increased trend for realistic reporting bases.
7.2 Risk premium reinsurance: The risk premium reinsurance method is one type of arrangement that has been associated
with a financing arrangement whereby the reinsurer relieves the cedant of part of its new
business financing requirement. A straightforward loan from the reinsurer would not
achieve the purpose, as the cedant would usually have to add the amount of the loan to its
So, the ‘loan’ is usually presented as a reinsurance commission related to the volume of
business reinsured. The ‘repayments’ – spread over a number of years – are added to the
reinsurance premiums. The reinsurer considers the expected lapse experience of the
portfolio of reinsurances in determining the loan repayments.
Essentially, all that is happening is that the reinsurer has increased its risk premium rates in future
years. In return for charging the insurer higher premiums in the future, the reinsurer is able to
offer the insurer a substantial initial commission. The ‘loan’ received over a period is therefore
equal to the total initial commissions payable under the arrangements over that time. As far as
repayments are concerned, the insurer simply agrees to pay more for its reinsurance protection
than it would normally have done.
7.3 Contingent loan: You may see this type of Fin Re referred to as surplus relief reinsurance.
An alternative approach makes use of the future profits contained in a block of new or
existing business.
Regulation will usually require the insurer to set aside assets (to cover reserves and solvency
capital requirements) that are well in excess of the realistic value of the liabilities. Over time, this
excess is expected to be returned to the insurer as profit.
These profits are often called the ‘VIF’ as they relate to the value in force, ie the value of the
profits from the in‐force business.
The reinsurer again provides a loan to the cedant, but as the repayment of the loan is
contingent upon the stream of future profits being generated by the business, the cedant
may not need to reserve for the repayment within its supervisory returns (depending on the
regulatory regime).
So, should the VIF profit not occur at all, the reinsurer would simply have made a capital gift to
the insurer equal to the amount of the loan. If the VIF profit does emerge, then the reinsurer will
receive all of the specified loan repayments.
Compare this with a normal, non‐contingent, loan. In this case, the assets would be increased by
the amount of the loan, but then the liabilities would have to increase as well to show the
expected cost of the future loan repayments. So, there would be no improvement to the amount
of surplus shown on the balance sheet.
However, with the contingent loan, the insurer does not have to show the value of the future loan
repayments as a liability in the balance sheet, as these will only materialise should the VIF profit
materialise (which is not shown as an asset).
8 Determination of the retention level: To determine the retention level to adopt, it is necessary first to estimate the statistical
distribution of the risk experience costs of the portfolio on various assumed retention
levels. The insurer then needs to judge how low a probability should be aimed at for
various degrees of departure from the overall average risk costs.
One approach is to set the retention level at such a level as to keep the probability of
insolvency (or ruin probability) below a specified level. Using a stochastic model for
expected claims rates and a model of the business, expected claims can be projected
forward together with the value of the company’s assets and liabilities. By using simulation
a retention level can then be determined such that the company stays solvent for 995, say,
\uf0b7 the less certain it is ab:          the lower is the acceptable probability of future insolvency
\uf0b7 the greater the variance: The size of the insurer’s free assets will have a major bearing on the reinsurance requirements as
far as claims volatility is concerned. In fact, many large insurers have sufficient free assets to be
able to dispense with XL reinsurance for normal policies, and will reinsure only exceptionally large
policies (eg £1 million sum at risk) and against catastrophes.
The size of the free assets will also obviously affect the insurer’s financing reinsurance
requirements – the more capital an insurer has, the less it needs capital assistance from
Another possible approach is to consider: (a)       the cost of financing an appropriate risk experience fluctuation reserve, and
(b)       the cost of obtaining reinsurance – the reinsurer naturally incorporates an expense
and profit loading in its reinsurance terms, and the cedant incurs administrative
As the retention level increases, (a) will increase and (b) will decrease, and a retention level
can be adopted that minimises the total (a) + (b).
To calculate (a) the simulation approach discussed above would probably need to be used
to determine the reserve that the company needs to hold.
Chapter 26 Summary: Reinsurance involves a direct‐writing company ceding business to another insurance
company, the reinsurer. The reinsurer will have no contact with the policyholders.
Types of reinsurance business: Reinsurance can be by treaty or facultative, proportional or non‐proportional (or financial).
Arrangements between cedant and reinsurer can be fac/fac, fac/ob or ob/ob.
Reinsurance commission: Reinsurance commission is paid from the reinsurer to the insurer. The commission may be
structured as a deduction from the reinsurance premium. This is known as a rebate.
For short\u2010term health business, onl: Under original terms, the reinsurance premiums are in the same proportion to the original
premium as the reinsured benefit is to the full benefit level. The reinsurer helps the finances
of the insurer by paying it commission. Deposits back may be used so that the cedant
maintains the reserves for the whole contract.
Chapter 26 Summary continued: Financial reinsurance typically involves very little transfer of insurance risk.
Financial reinsurance is only effective under supervisory regimes where credit cannot be
taken for the insurer’s future profits, and/or where a realistic liability does not have to be
held in respect of the loan repayments.
For risk premium reinsurance:          a proportion of either the full benefit or (sometimes) the sum at risk is reinsured
         reinsurance premiums can be net level payments or yearly increasing
\uf0b7 reinsurance premiums are: Risks are shared in proportions. For quota share this proportion is the same for all risks, but
\uf0b7 financing and improving s: Surplus reinsurance enables the insurer to cover individual risks up to an agreed maximum
retention level. This limits the insurer’s concentration of risk on individual policies and so
Non\u2010proportional reinsurance: Non‐proportional reinsurance helps to cap claims costs, and is normally only used in short‐
term contracts and possibly group long‐term contracts. It is also used to smooth results by
It can be:          risk excess of loss, which relates to individual losses and may be found on PMI
         aggregate excess of loss (stop loss) , which covers aggregations of claims over a
whole account and is rare for long‐term insurance
         catastrophe excess of loss, which covers events or pandemics.
Financial reinsurance: Financial reinsurance can help the cedant to relieve part of its new business financing
requirement. It can be structured like a loan, receiving either a lump sum or reinsurance
commissions with repayments incorporated into the reinsurance premium or paid out of
Determining the retention level: The retention level may be determined by estimating the distribution of claim costs under
various retentions, and then picking one that gives a suitably low probability of adverse net
experience. This could be based on insolvency (or ruin) probabilities using a stochastic
The model can be used to find the mix of reinsurance and of holding a risk experience
fluctuation reserve that minimises the cost of the claim fluctuation protection.
